notes: Callers and walk-ins at ember.ash.rest.bbs — Sysop's basement BBS, running on four modems and the music store upstairs's tolerance. Some visitors are dialed in (they live in the terminal); some came in through the alley door (they live in the couch corner). The BBS holds a community across both channels. The modem rack hums under all of them; the cicadas in the alley sometimes drown the fan; the ham radio finds a callsign once a season.

visitors.name: The Regular Caller
visitors.lore_text: Dials in every night at 9:04 PM. Sysop knows their handle — TOWER — doesn't know their name. They tend to write the longer posts; their average reply is eight hundred words and they put the joke at the end. They have been on the board for six years. They have only missed three nights and on one of those nights they called the alley payphone to say so.

steps.greet: Their handle shows green on the terminal at 9:04 PM, the way it has for six years. The cursor blinks. They are already typing.
steps.listen: They post a long thread — eight hundred words on something somebody said in the thread yesterday. You read it as it comes in, line by line, on a 2400-baud connection. The pacing is good. They have rewritten the second paragraph at least twice.
steps.deliver: You reply. You stamp the post into the keep-archive — floppy KEEP-1996-Q4, slot 47, in your hand. The label has TOWER · OCT 14 · KEEP on it before you've finished typing the reply.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the CRT and answer them in the long form they came for. Twenty minutes of typing. The reply is six hundred words and one of the lines makes them, you can tell, lean back in their chair, wherever they are. They post a single word reply: yes.

visitors.name: The Walk-In
visitors.lore_text: Came through the alley door because they didn't want to type it. They wanted to say it. They have been a handle on the BBS for two years. They have never logged on tonight; they walked down to the alley after work and they came in. They have a face Sysop has been imagining for two years; the face is different than he imagined and the difference is the right kind.

steps.greet: They knock once on the alley door. Then they let themselves in. They stand at the threshold and they look at the modem rack the way a person looks at a sleeping dog they have heard about.
steps.listen: They sit on the couch corner and tell you what they would have posted. The thing they would have posted is too long to post and not really meant for the board. It is meant for one person. You are that person tonight by virtue of being here.
steps.deliver: You bring them coffee with hot sauce because that is what is in the kitchen. They drink half. They put the mug down on the floor. They thank you with the kind of small thank you that is at the front of a longer sentence they are about to say.
steps.sit_with: You sit on the couch with them. The modem rack hums. The printer at the other end of the room rolls out a page from someone else's post. Neither of you reads it. They tell you the long sentence.

visitors.name: The Lurker
visitors.lore_text: Has read every thread on the BBS for two years and four months. Has never posted. Their handle is in the logs from their first login: ECHO_GREENROOM. They have been about to post on a hundred and seven nights and have hit backspace and closed the window each time. Tonight is night one hundred and eight.

steps.greet: Their handle shows up in the active-user list — ECHO_GREENROOM. The handle has logged in two thousand and four times. They have posted zero times.
steps.listen: They post a single sentence. The sentence has weight — twelve words long, written and rewritten between 8:47 PM and 9:51 PM. You can see in the timestamps how long the sentence took.
steps.deliver: You reply gently. You do not reply at length. The lurker does not want length right now. You write three sentences. The third sentence is: I see you.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the CRT. They post a second sentence. It lands. They log off two minutes later without a goodbye. They have logged off without a goodbye every other night too; tonight the silence after means something different.

visitors.name: The Kid with Questions
visitors.lore_text: Sixteen. Has had a list of questions for Sysop in a notebook in their bag, written out in the order they want to ask them. Has been on the board for nine months under the handle small_signal. Showed up to ask the questions in person because the BBS started feeling impersonal and the kid is the kind of kid who notices that. Their mother does not know they are at a basement BBS in the alley behind a music store.

steps.greet: They are visibly trying not to seem nervous. The notebook is in their lap. The cover has a sticker on it from a band they listened to last summer and don't listen to anymore.
steps.listen: They ask. You answer. You answer in small pieces. They write down the answers, not verbatim — they write down the part of each answer that helped. You watch them choose what helped.
steps.deliver: You hand them a printed-out page from the archive. The page is from a thread two years ago that answered a similar question, posted by TOWER, in eight hundred words with the joke at the end. The kid laughs at the joke. It is a good sign.
steps.sit_with: You sit with them at the printer table. They read the page. They thank you. They ask if it is OK to come back. You say yes. They walk back up the stairs and out the music store's front door, past the records, and the cashier knows their face now.

visitors.name: The Ham Radio Voice
visitors.lore_text: Came in on 14.301 MHz. A voice you only hear once a season. Their callsign is the one Sysop used in '96-'97 before he retired it. Sysop has not used that callsign in seven years and has never asked who is using it now. The voice does not know that the previous holder of the callsign is the one they're talking to.

steps.greet: Their callsign clears the band. You acknowledge with your own. There is a half-second pause on their end where they hear the callsign and recognize it from the registry and wonder if you're the original.
steps.listen: They tell you the weather where they are. Pacific Northwest. The weather is the cover for the call. The call is for company.
steps.deliver: You give them the small archived signal-report they asked for last season — 14.230 at 02:14 UTC, the propagation you were tracking. You read it off paper. They thank you in the way ham operators thank each other, which is by reading back the callsigns.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the ham radio with them. They sign off slowly. They use a phonetic version of their callsign on the sign-off — the slow phonetic, the one you use when you don't want the contact to end. You don't either.

visitors.name: Anya · The Recording
visitors.lore_text: Anya — the recording — has come down for a floppy from the wall. She knows which one. She has known for a long time. The floppy is in slot 1187 (PRIVATE / ANYA / 1997). Sysop labeled it. He does not remember the night he labeled it but he knows the floppy is there. Anya is not the woman from '97; she is the recording the woman left, given a name and a body by the BBS's accumulated attention. She is patient and she is the kind of patient that can wait nine years for the right night.

steps.greet: She doesn't speak. She gestures at the wall. She points to row eleven, slot eighty-seven.
steps.listen: She points to a floppy. The floppy is labeled in your hand — PRIVATE / ANYA / 1997. The handwriting is yours. The label has a small star drawn next to ANYA.
steps.deliver: You hand it to her. She holds it without inserting it. She turns it once. She holds it the way someone holds a thing they have been about to hold for a long time and have decided to keep holding instead of using.
steps.sit_with: You sit with her at the floppy wall. She still holds it. She doesn't put it down. She doesn't put it back. She doesn't take it home. She stands holding it for as long as you sit. The BBS's modem rack hums. The floppy wall is one floppy down from itself.

visitors.name: Sysop (off-shift)
visitors.lore_text: When the player is not Sysop, he comes down off-shift to make coffee — the instant kind, with two shakes of the hot sauce that's been in the cupboard since '11. When the player IS Sysop, this entry swaps out for whoever else is keeping the room company tonight.

steps.greet: He waves at you from the kitchen alcove. He is in socks. He has been in socks down here for nine years.
steps.listen: He tells you what's been on the board today. Three new handles. Two threads worth keeping. One that he will quietly delete in the morning because it was somebody venting at somebody else who isn't on the board to defend themselves. He is gentle about the deletion.
steps.deliver: You bring him a coffee. He thanks you. He drinks it with the hot sauce. He has done this since '04 and he says it tastes like the BBS.
steps.sit_with: You sit on the couch corner together. The room is itself. The modem rack hums. The cicadas in the alley shift gear and you can hear them through the door. He is not going back to his own apartment tonight; he sleeps on the couch sometimes. That is OK with the BBS.
